coredata.dc:title: Table sugar derived carbon nanodots for the extraction of bulk silver
coredata.dc:creator: Ansi, V.A.
Renuka, N.K.
coredata.dc:description: Reducing nature of table sugar derived carbon dots, achieved via an economic and environmentally benign procedure is presented in the article. The as-synthesized carbon dots are adequately characterized using Transmission Electron Microscopy, X-Ray Diffraction analysis, as well as Fourier Transform Infra-Red, X-ray photoelectron, and Fluorescence spectroscopies. The particles possess average particle size of 3.8 nm, with UV absorption maximum at 245 nm, along with shoulder band around 320 nm. Bright cyan emission upon UV irradiation is also noted for the system. The commendable reducing nature of the system is illustrated through the conversion of Ag (I) to Ag(0) in bulk metallic form, via an extremely facile way.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
Carbon nano dots
Luminescence
Reducing ability
Silver extraction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon nanodots for extraction of metallic silver

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Ansi V.A. acknowledges the financial assistance received from the Government of Kerala . We are grateful to the Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut for providing the research facilities. The authors also acknowledge STIC, CUSAT for providing the HRTEM images.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM image of carbon dots (a); particle size distribution derived from TEM analysis (b); XRD pattern (c); UV–visible absorbance curve (d); excitation dependant photo-luminescence spectra (e) and the fluorescence decay curve (f) at excitation at 370 nm.
FTIR spectrum (a); XPS spectral profiles of carbon dot: C1s (b), O1s (c); Raman spectral profile of the sample (d).
Digital images of Ag (I) solution after treatment with carbon dots, at different durations. 15 min (a); 3 h (b); 20 h (c); glass vessel with silver coating (d).

head.ce:title: Table sugar derived carbon nanodots for the extraction of bulk silver

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala 673 635, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala 673 635, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reducing nature of table sugar derived carbon dots, achieved via an economic and environmentally benign procedure is presented in the article. The as-synthesized carbon dots are adequately characterized using Transmission Electron Microscopy, X-Ray Diffraction analysis, as well as Fourier Transform Infra-Red, X-ray photoelectron, and Fluorescence spectroscopies. The particles possess average particle size of 3.8 nm, with UV absorption maximum at 245 nm, along with shoulder band around 320 nm. Bright cyan emission upon UV irradiation is also noted for the system. The commendable reducing nature of the system is illustrated through the conversion of Ag (I) to Ag(0) in bulk metallic form, via an extremely facile way.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon has always been a fascinating element, that claims numerous applications in various walks of life. Several allotropic forms, as well as their composites serve as promising candidates in different fields of science and technology [1,2]. Carbon nanodot, the quasi-spherical fluorescent particle, with size falling below 10 nm, is one among the most exploited nano materials in recent time. The intriguing feature possessed by carbon nanodots, fluorescence, and the applications thus conferred, are subjected to extensive research [3–9]. Carbon dots are extensively utilized for the fabrication of numerous optical sensors for metal ions, organic compounds, bio-molecules etc. [5–9]. Apart from the luminescence, its non-toxic and bio-compatible nature are also exploited widely, which render them potent candidates in bio-medical field [9,10]. In the present report, we discuss the reducing nature of carbon dots, one of the less explored features of the system. Few works are noted in literature, wherein carbon dots assist in the synthesis of metal nanoparticles from their respective salt solutions [11–14]. The reducing ability of carbon dots has been attributed to surface oxygen functionalities present on the carbon core of the system.

Herein, we demonstrate the commendable reducing ability of table sugar derived carbon nanodots, in aiding the extraction of metallic silver from Ag (I) solution, under considerably mild conditions (~100 °C). Among the transition metals, silver, one of the seven metals of antiquity, has grabbed pronounced significance for its multifaceted applications. Utilisation of the reducing ability of carbon particles to yield Ag (0) in nano dimension has been attempted previously. Action of carbon dots as reducing as well as stabilising agents in the said reaction has been reported by Jin et al. [15]. Though this procedure highlights the dual role of the system, the route includes complicated reaction procedure involving a number of chemicals like acetylcholine chloride, mercaptosuccinic acid, N-acetyl-L-cysteine, oleic acid etc., along with considerably high temperature and post-synthesis treatments. Other similar works also demanded comparatively higher temperatures and long duration [16–18]. Jin and group presented a modified version of their own procedure for the fabrication of silver nano particles [19] which involved ammonia, isopropanol etc. It is obvious that compared to these previous works, the present report offers a greener and milder route for affecting Ag(I) to Ag(0) conversion. A significant achievement made here is the extraction of bulk silver. Generally, smelting/chemical leaching of silver ores is conducted for the extraction of bulk quantities of metallic silver [20]. Among the various metallurgical extraction processes, cyanide process using argentite ore is the widely accepted and practised method [21], which uses tedious multistep procedures, and involve electrolytic purification techniques. Though the process yields quantitative extraction of silver from the abundantly available ore argentite, usage of large amount of toxic sodium cyanide and tedious purification processes are regarded as the shortcomings of the method. Hence, an innovative move in the direction of extracting silver in bulk form from the precursor salt solution is highly solicited.

Table sugar was collected from the local market (Thenjipalam, Malappuram), and 90% ammonia solution was purchased from Merck. Distilled water was used for the dilution purposes throughout the synthesis procedure. In a typical synthesis process, 1 g of table sugar was subjected to caramelization at 150 °C for 3 min in a preheated muffle furnace. The caramelized sugar syrup thus obtained was allowed to cool normally to room temperature, followed by mixing with diluted 20 mL 90% ammonia solution (1:10 vol ratio). The solution was further subjected to micro-wave treatment (Monowave 300 synthesis microwave oven – Anton Paar) for 3 min at 120 °C maintaining power at 3 W (reaction parameters were chosen after a series of trial and error experiments by altering each experimental parameter), at which perfect crystal growth and nucleation were believed to occur during carbon dot formation. Maximum fluorescence intensity was noted only at this specified condition. The resultant solution was subjected to ultra-centrifugation for 20 min at the speed rate of 12,000 rpm and the purity of the prepared carbon nanodots were further ensured by conducting dialysis for 24 h against distilled water.

HR-TEM experiment werewas conducted with the aid of Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscope. XRD analysis was conducted using Rigaku MiniFlex- II diffractometer using Cu K(α) radiation. JASCO V-550 spectrophotometer was used to obtain the absorbance characteristics of the system. The FTIR spectral data were achieved using FTIR-4100 instrument. UV light assisted studies were carried out using LZC 4X UV reactor. Perkin Elmer Fluorescence spectrometer LS 55 served to record the Fluorescence feature and the Fluorescence life time of sample was measured using Fluoro Log-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer - Jobin Yvon Inc., USA (Exc. wavelength at 370 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the sample was carried out using Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer with Al Kα X-ray beam source. Raman spectrum of the sample was recorded using LabRam HR-Horiba Jobinyvon Spectrometer using Raman Microprobe with 532 nm Nd:YAG excitation source.

Formation of quasi-spherical particles with size ~ 5 nm is evident from the HRTEM (Fig. 1 a). Lattice fringes with ‘d’ spacing of 0.21 nm are evident (inset). Particle size distribution of the system (Fig. 1b) reveals the average size of carbon particles to be 3.8 nm. XRD pattern showed characteristic peaks at 2θ values 24° and 44.5°, corresponding to (002) and (101) planes of carbon dots, in the respective order (Fig. 1c). UV–Visible absorbance characteristics typical for carbon dots is provided in Fig. 1(d). The system exhibited typical luminescence which was excitation dependant (Fig. 1e). The exact structure as well as the luminescence are the two unresolved problems regarding carbon dots. Researchers suggest that the structural “inhomogeneity” of carbon dots is the reason for the excitation wavelength dependant emission behaviour of the system. In addition to these structural defects, the emission form various electronic states are also suggested as the reason behind this property. Polydispersity of the systems as well as the significant role of various surface functional groups also make solid reasons for the excitation dependant emission behaviour. Carbon dots exhibited bright cyan emission when excited at 360 nm. Fig. 1f presents the fluorescence decay curve of carbon dots, with fluorescence life time 4.9 ns.

Presence of epoxy, carboxyl, and hydroxyl functionalities in the system was confirmed from the FTIR bands at ~1000 cm−1, 1700 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1 respectively (Fig. 2 a). Further confirmation on the existence of these surface anchored groups is achieved from the XPS data (Fig. 2b and 2c). The percentages of Carbon and Oxygen in the system are determined to be 34.59 and 65.41 respectively. More details about the structural aspects of the system are obtained from the Raman spectral profile (Fig. 2d), which support the observations from XPS and FTIR spectroscopy results. The system exhibited two distinct bands at 1298 cm−1 (D-band) and 1597 cm1 (G-band), characteristic of carbon dots. The G band accounts for the in-plane vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms on the carbon core, while the former is attributed to out of plane breathing vibrations of carbon atoms. The intensity ratio of these two bands (ID/IG) was found to exceed unity, which confirms the presence of carbon core that is heavily decorated with surface functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanodots for extraction of metallic silver
ce:section.ce:para: A plausible structure assigned to carbon dots is provided in Fig. 3 . The large amount of oxygen functionalities in the system imparts excellent reducing power to carbon dots [11–14,22–27]. Here, the reducing ability is utilised for the successful extraction of metallic silver, Ag (0) from silver nitrate, Ag (I) solution. For this, about 100 µL of 0.05 M silver nitrate solution is added into 5 mL of boiling distilled water, to which10 µL of carbon dot solution is added with stirring. The solution is then cooled to room temperature. The solution turns into reddish brown within 15 min, and after 3 h, into black. Upon storage for about 20 h, the inner side of glass bottle gets covered with a shiny mirror coating, confirming the deposition of metallic silver inside the glass vessel. Digital images of the solution showing these sequential changes are displayed in Fig. 4 . The experiment was repeated several times, and was found to be reproducible, confirming the practicability of the procedure. The decisive role of carbon dots in the reduction process is confirmed through various trial and error experiments (Supplementary information).

Previous reports authenticate that oxygen functional groups (C=O and C–OH), on the carbon core are instrumental behind this reducing ability of carbon dots. The specific convesion of Ag+ to Ag0 is attributed to oxygen containing surface groups, as reported by Ghosal et al., who suggested the significant role of –OH and –COOH groups in the reduction process [16]. According to Jin et al., oxygen containing surface groups is the sole factor that brings the reduction of silver ions giving silver nanoparticles [19]. This is supported by the absence of -COOH groups as well as the dramatic decrease in C-OH content in fitted XPS (C 1 s) of silver nano crystals. Masoud and group relate the reducing action of carbon nanodots with oxygen bearing functional groups [25]. The hydroxyl groups present over the surface of carbon dots act as reducing centres, which is accompanied by the simultaneous increase in C=O (carbonyl) groups, at the expense of –OH moieties.

Cyan emitting carbon nanodots were prepared through a facile and economic route from table sugar. The structure of the particles was confirmed through various characterisation techniques. Excellent reducing ability was noted for the system, which is established through the reduction of Ag(I) to Ag(0). The reducing ability was attributed to oxygen functionalities present on the carbon core.
V.A. Ansi: Investigation, Writing - original draft. N.K. Renuka: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ansi V.A. acknowledges the financial assistance received from the Government of Kerala. We are grateful to the Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut for providing the research facilities. The authors also acknowledge STIC, CUSAT for providing the HRTEM images.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.128985.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic anisotropy of the graphite nanoplatelet-epoxy and MWCNT-epoxy composites with aligned barium ferrite filler

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O.S. Yakovenko, L.Yu. Matzui, L.L. Vovchenko, A.V. Trukhanov, I.S. Kazakevich, S.V. Trukhanov, Y.I. Prylutskyy, U. Ritter, Magnetic anisotropy of the graphite nanoplatelet-epoxy and MWCNT-epoxy composites with aligned barium ferrite filler, J. Mat. Sci. 52 (2017) 5345-5358.

Trukhanov, Electrophysical properties of epoxy-based composites with graphite nanoplatelets and magnetically aligned magnetite, Mol. Cryst. Liq. Cryst. 661 (2018) 68-80.
S.N. Baker, G.A. Baker, Luminescent carbon nanodots: emergent nanolights, Angew Chem Int Ed. 49 (2010) 6726–6744.
